title: Vocabulary
objective: Students will explore vocabulary concepts including Discussing and Questioning New Words, Sorting & Categorising Words, Shades of Meaning and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Discussing and Questioning New Words? Let's find out together!
Ask and answer questions about unknown words in texts; discuss word meanings and link new vocabulary to words already known
Let's practice together! Ask 'What does ___ mean?' when meeting unfamiliar words during reading
When you comes across a word they don't know in a book, do they try to figure out what it means from the other words around it and connect it to words they already know?

Great job! Today we learned about Discussing and Questioning New Words. Ask 'What does ___ mean?' when meeting unfamiliar words during reading Use context and pictures to work out what a new word might mean
Let's explore Sorting & Categorising Words! Have you ever wondered about this?
Sort common objects and words into categories to understand how concepts relate; demonstrate understanding of opposites (antonyms) for common verbs and adjectives
Let me show you something cool. Group words or objects by category (e.g. animals, foods, colours)
Can you sort a group of words into categories — for example, separating fruits from vegetables — and tell you the opposite of common words like "hot" or "fast"?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Sorting & Categorising Words: Group words or objects by category (e.g. animals, foods, colours) Identify and produce opposite pairs (e.g. 'big/small', 'hot/cold', 'go/stop')
Have you ever thought about Shades of Meaning? Let's find out together!
Distinguish shades of meaning among verbs describing similar actions and among adjectives differing in intensity; make real-life connections between words and their use
Let me show you something cool. Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance')
Does you notice the difference between similar words — for example, knowing that "scorching" is stronger than "warm," or that "trotted" is different from "sprinted"?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Shades of Meaning: Compare similar verbs by acting them out (e.g. 'walk', 'march', 'strut', 'prance') Order adjectives by intensity (e.g. big, huge, gigantic)
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Using New Vocabulary!
Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, being read to, and responding to texts in own speech and writing
Let's practice together! Incorporate new vocabulary from read-alouds into conversations
When you learns a new word from a book or conversation, do they try to use it themselves — in their writing or when they're talking?
Great job! Today we learned about Using New Vocabulary. Incorporate new vocabulary from read-alouds into conversations Attempt to use interesting or topic-specific words in own writing
Let's discover the amazing world of Word Parts as Clues!
Use knowledge of common inflections and affixes (-ed, -s, un-, -er) as clues to the meaning of unknown words; understand how the prefix un- changes meaning
Time to try this out: Recognise that -ed signals past tense (e.g. 'jumped' = already happened)
If you sees an unfamiliar word like "unhelpful" or "jumped," can they use the beginning or ending of the word to work out what it means?

Great job! Today we learned about Word Parts as Clues. Recognise that -ed signals past tense (e.g. 'jumped' = already happened) Explain how un- makes a word mean the opposite (e.g. 'unhappy' = not happy)
